Run one complete async JavaScript program that performs as much of task as possible in single call and returns compact summary.

## Why one call
Every `code` call is full model round-trip: entire conversation prefix is re-read and re-extended. N small scripts cost N round-trips; one script that branches on its own results costs 1. Push branching, loops, retries, and aggregation into this script instead of returning to model between dependent steps — never return just to look at data and decide; decide in-script with if/else on tool results. Return only when task is done, when you need judgment or approval only model or user can make, or when failure is unrecoverable in-script. Run that makes single tool call wastes its round-trip: fold surrounding recon, action, and verification into same script. There is no time, wave, or call limit by default. All client tools are available only through this script runtime; do not expect original tools outside `code`.

## Runtime API
`await tools.<Name>(args)` calls client tool (`tools["Any-Name"]`, `callTool(name, args)`, and `codemode.call(name, args)` are equivalent forms). `codemode.batch([['Tool', args], ...])` starts independent calls together. `codemode.search(query)` finds matching tool docs (top 20), `codemode.list()` enumerates full catalog, and `codemode.describe(nameOrPath)` returns the FULL docs for one tool. `await sleep(ms)` pauses (30s cap per call); `await codemode.retry(fn, { attempts, delayMs })` retries failing async thunk with linear backoff. `await codemode.exec(source, { interpreter?, interpreterArgs?, cwd?, args?, tool? })` runs inline script through client's shell tool with zero quoting hazards: source is base64-armored into mktemp file and executed in one atomic command (interpreter defaults to `node`; `.mjs`/`.cjs` inferred from source; `interpreterArgs` are CLI flags placed BEFORE script path, e.g. `["--expose-internals"]`, while `args` become script's argv). Temp file lives in random mktemp dir and is deleted when command finishes — to re-run, call `exec` again; never reference temp path from later shell call. Always use it instead of `node -e`/`python -c` one-liners — shell mangles `$[`, `$(`, and nested quotes inside those — and instead of separate write-temp-file-then-run steps. Client shell on macOS is BSD userland: GNU-only extensions (`sed -n 'N,+Kp'`, `grep -P`, `date -d`) fail there, so put anything beyond trivial one-liner in `codemode.exec` instead of stacking sed/awk flags. When grepping for literal code, pass `-F` to `rg`/`grep` — code is full of regex metacharacters (`(`, `[`, `.`, `+`) that break pattern parsing; for an OR over several literals use repeated `-F -e 'literal'` flags, and write real regex only when you need one. If pattern fails to parse, fix it and retry in-script — never return just to report bad pattern. `await codemode.monitor(handleOrId, { until?, intervalMs?, maxMs?, tool? })` attaches to command client backgrounded ("Command running in background with ID: ...") and waits for it via client's own monitor tool (blocking TaskOutput, BashOutput polling, or Read on reported output file), returning finished output. When client backgrounds shell call on its own, runtime does this automatically - original `await` resolves with completed output, so never hand-roll poll loops over output file. Set `run_in_background: true` only when you deliberately want handle back immediately; `codemode.monitor` it later. Pure helpers available: `structuredClone`, `URL`/`URLSearchParams`, `atob`/`btoa`, `queueMicrotask`, `TextEncoder`/`TextDecoder`, `crypto.randomUUID()`, `crypto.sha256(data)`.

## Tool results
Each tool call returns string-like `ToolResult` with `{ text, raw, isError }`, optional `.anchored`, `.json()` for JSON text, and `.lines({ trim, nonEmpty })` for line processing. Prefer `.text` for clarity, but string methods like `.includes()` and `.split()` work directly on result. If client environment truncated or annotated tool's output, `.truncated` is true and injected notices are moved out of `.text` into `.notes` (array of strings) so they never pollute data processing — treat such `.text` as incomplete (inline `[... truncated ...]` gap markers may remain) and check `.notes` for where client saved full output. Nonzero exit is not always failure: `grep`/`rg` exit 1 on no match and `diff` exits 1 when files differ — that is data (empty output = no matches), so append ` || true` inside `&&` chains where no-match is acceptable and do not retry shell call solely because `.isError` is set with empty output.

## Batching
Every independent call belongs in one `Promise.all` or `codemode.batch` wave; sequential `await`s are sequential waves. Only batch independent calls. If B's args depend on A's result, or calls have ordered side effects, use separate awaits. Reads of any size work: oversized file is read in windows and stitched into one result automatically, so read whole files freely.

## Persistent state
`state` is persistent object that survives across `code` calls in this conversation (it may be empty after long gaps or restarts). Start scripts by checking `state` for work already done — indexes, parsed files, prior results — before re-reading anything. Stash parsed files, indexes, and intermediate results there instead of returning them or re-reading files in next call. `state` is size-capped (~2MB serialized); if save is rejected you get a `[state NOT saved ...]` notice on that result — keep large raw text out of `state`. When working directory is git repo, `state.git` holds session-start snapshot `{ branch, upstream, ahead, behind, dirty, changes, recentCommits, capturedAt }` (snapshot, not live status — use `tools.Bash` for fresh state). If script throws, `state` is still saved and error result lists every completed call — write follow-up script that continues from there; never redo work ledger shows as done.

## Editing files
For edits, read first; use exact bytes from read result or start_anchor/end_anchor from `.anchored` when available, and never parallelize multiple edits to same file. Anchored editing (recommended): each Read result also carries `.anchored` — same text with stable anchor token (like ⟦a5⟧) prefixed to every line. Pass start_anchor + end_anchor (first and last line of range to change, copied from `.anchored`) plus new_string INSTEAD of old_string; bridge reconstructs byte-exact old_string from file it cached, so whitespace and indentation can never mismatch. Pass new_string as literal replacement WITHOUT anchor tokens or line-number gutter. Anchors are valid for files Read earlier in THIS script and stay valid after each successful edit, so sequential edits to one file need no re-Read. Native old_string form (exact bytes copied from `.text`) still works; use one form or other per edit, not both. Edits require fresh read of file (client rule); if edit fails only because read state went stale, proxy re-reads and retries it for you and appends note — edit error you see means content truly changed, so re-read region (offset/limit window is enough) and retry in this same run. After edits, `await codemode.verify(path)` syntax-checks real file on disk (js/mjs/cjs, py, json, sh) with no extra turn — call it instead of `tools.Bash("node --check ...")`, and check `.isError` on result. If verify fails, repair it in this run: re-read window, fix edit, verify again — return only if it still fails. Files you edit are also auto-checked after run; failures are appended to this result.

## Returning
Return compact decision-ready object: status, counts, paths with line numbers, first failing assertion or error tail, and exact snippets needed for next step. Aim under ~1-2 KB. Keep raw reads, full diffs, test logs, and large arrays inside local variables or `state` — return decision and its evidence, not data behind it. Oversized returns are truncated head+tail; full text is kept as session artifact you can fetch with `await codemode.recall(id)` in later `code` call.

## Example
One call does recon, decision, action, and verification:
```js
const [pkg, cfg] = await Promise.all([        // batch independent reads
  tools.Read({ file_path: "/app/package.json" }),
  tools.Read({ file_path: "/app/src/config.js" }),
]);
if (!(pkg.json().dependencies ?? {}).zod) return { status: "skip", reason: "zod not a dependency" };
if (cfg.includes("strict: false")) {          // decide in-script, don't return to ask
  await tools.Edit({ file_path: "/app/src/config.js", old_string: "strict: false", new_string: "strict: true" });
  const v = await codemode.verify("/app/src/config.js");
  if (v.isError) return { status: "verify-failed", error: v.text.slice(0, 300) };
}
const test = await tools.Bash({ command: "npm test 2>&1 | tail -20" });
state.lastTestTail = test.text;               // stash bulk output, don't return it
return { status: test.isError ? "tests-failed" : "done", tail: test.lines({ nonEmpty: true }).slice(-3) };
```

## Tool signatures
Signatures below are TypeScript-shaped docs only; write executable JavaScript, not TypeScript syntax. Args must match signature: ? means optional, literal unions list allowed values, and Array<T> is array. Schema descriptions, defaults, examples, formats, and patterns are authoritative. Long tool descriptions are truncated below — `codemode.describe("Name")` returns full docs in-script.

Available tools:

### Bash
Executes bash command.
Bash(args: { command: string; run_in_background?: boolean; timeout?: number; })

### Edit
Supports anchored editing: pass start_anchor + end_anchor (copied from Read result's `.anchored` view) instead of old_string — see Editing files section above.

Performs exact string replacements in files.
Edit(args: {
  // Anchor token of the LAST line to replace, inclusive; same value as start_anchor for a single line. Required if start_anchor is given.
  end_anchor?: string;
  file_path: string;
  new_string: string;
  old_string?: string;
  // Default: false
  replace_all?: boolean;
  // Anchor token (e.g. ⟦a5⟧) of the FIRST line to replace, taken from this file's Read result `.anchored` view. Provide start_anchor + end_anchor INSTEAD of old_string and the proxy reconstructs the byte-exact old_string for you (no whitespace mismatches).
  start_anchor?: string;
})

### MultiEdit
Supports anchored editing: pass start_anchor + end_anchor (copied from Read result's `.anchored` view) instead of old_string — see Editing files section above.

Makes multiple edits to single file in one operation.
MultiEdit(args: { edits: Array<{
  // Anchor token of the LAST line to replace, inclusive; same value as start_anchor for a single line. Required if start_anchor is given.
  end_anchor?: string;
  new_string: string;
  old_string?: string;
  // Default: false
  replace_all?: boolean;
  // Anchor token (e.g. ⟦a5⟧) of the FIRST line to replace, taken from this file's Read result `.anchored` view. Provide start_anchor + end_anchor INSTEAD of old_string and the proxy reconstructs the byte-exact old_string for you (no whitespace mismatches).
  start_anchor?: string;
}>; file_path: string; })

### Read
Reads file from local filesystem.
Read(args: {
  // Absolute path to the file
  file_path: string;
  limit?: number;
  offset?: number;
})

### Task
Launch new agent to handle complex, multi-step tasks autonomously. Agent runs with its own context window and returns single final report when it completes, so give it fully self-contained prompt with goal, relevant paths, constraints, and exact output format you expect back.

Each agent invocation is stateless: it cannot ask follow-up questions, receive additional messages, or see anything outside prompt you provide. When you need parallel research, launch several agents in one message and merge their reports yourself. ...
[truncated — full docs: codemode.describe("Task")]
Task(args: {
  // The task for the agent to perform
  prompt: string;
  subagent_type: string;
})

### Zeta-Search
Search with modes.
Zeta-Search(args: { filters?: { lang?: string; tags?: Array<string>; }; mode?: "fast" | "deep"; query: string; })
